## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Industrial Engineering — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Industrial Engineering (IE)* is the branch of engineering concerned with the **design, improvement, and installation of integrated systems** of **people, materials, equipment, and energy**. It draws upon specialized knowledge and skills in the mathematical, physical, and social sciences, together with the principles and methods of engineering analysis and design, to specify, predict, and evaluate the results to be obtained from such systems. (Source: AIIE / IIE Global Definition, universally adopted in KTU syllabus.)

**In simple terms** — Imagine you own a small bakery that makes bread. The flour, the oven, the baker, and the electricity are your *inputs*. The loaves of bread are your *outputs*. An Industrial Engineer is the person who studies this whole bakery system and asks:
- *Why is the baker walking 5 extra steps for flour?* (Motion study)
- *Can we arrange ovens in a U-shape to save time?* (Plant layout)
- *Will a bigger mixer reduce 2 hours of work daily?* (Work measurement)
- *Should we open a second branch in the city or a nearby town?* (Plant location)

That is Industrial Engineering — **making the entire system more efficient, productive, and human-friendly.**

---

### 1.2 History and Development of Industrial Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> Industrial Engineering did not appear overnight. It evolved across **three distinct waves** of human industrial thought — from the *Industrial Revolution* (18th c.) to *Scientific Management* (early 20th c.) to *Modern Systems Era* (post-WWII).

#### 1.2.1 Pre-Industrial Engineering Era (1771–1880)
- **1771** – Richard Arkwright opened the first *water-powered cotton spinning mill* in Cromford, England — the start of factory mechanization.
- The division of labour was popularized by **Adam Smith** (*The Wealth of Nations*, 1776) — pin manufacturing example.
- **Charles Babbage** extended Smith's work in *On the Economy of Machinery and Manufactures* (1832), proposing that *division of labour saves time* and depends on worker skill.

#### 1.2.2 Scientific Management Era (1880–1940) — The True Birth of IE
This is the most exam-relevant period. Memorize the **"Pioneers Table"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pioneers of Scientific Management (High-Yield KTU Table):**

| Year | Pioneer | Contribution |
|------|---------|--------------|
| 1895 | **Frederick W. Taylor** | Father of Scientific Management; *Time Study; Differential Piece Wage System* |
| 1901 | **F.W. Taylor** | Shop Management (published) |
| 1903 | **F.W. Taylor** | Added "**Stop-watch method**" — Time measurement |
| 1911 | **F.W. Taylor** | *The Principles of Scientific Management* (book) |
| 1885 | **Henry L. Gantt** | *Gantt Chart* (Task & Bonus Wage System) |
| 1898 | **Frank B. Gilbreth** | *Motion Study* (Therbligs) |
| 1900s | **Lillian Gilbreth** | *Micro-motion study*; fatigue & human factors |
| 1900s | **Harrington Emerson** | *Efficiency*; 12 Principles of Efficiency; "**The Father of Efficiency Engineering**" |
| 1913 | **Henry Ford** | First **moving assembly line** (Model T) |
| 1913 | **Henry Ford** | *$5/day* wage — personnel management |
| 1920s | **Walter Shewhart** | *Statistical Quality Control (SQC)* |
| 1930s | **L.H.C. Tippett** | Sampling techniques in QC |

#### 1.2.3 Modern Industrial Engineering Era (1940–Present)
- **WWII** — Operations Research (OR) emerged for military logistics, radar, convoy routing.
- **Post-1945** — Integration of OR, ergonomics, computers, and cybernetics.
- **1950s–60s** — Systems Engineering, Human Factors Engineering, value engineering by **Larry Miles**.
- **1970s–80s** — Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM), MRP, JIT (Toyota Production System by **Taiichi Ohno**).
- **1990s–2000s** — Lean Manufacturing, Six Sigma (Motorola, **Bill Smith**), ERP, TQM.
- **2010s–Today** — Industry 4.0, IIoT, AI-driven optimization, Digital Twins, sustainable/green IE.

---

### 1.3 Role of an Industrial Engineer

> [!NOTE]
> An Industrial Engineer works at the **intersection of engineering, management, and human behaviour**. They are the *systems optimizer* of any organization.

**Primary Roles (KTU Expected Answer):**

1. **Process & System Designer** — Designs production and service systems.
2. **Productivity Improver** — Eliminates waste (muda) and inefficiency.
3. **Work Measurement Specialist** — Conducts time study, motion study, work sampling.
4. **Quality Controller** — Applies SQC, Six Sigma, TQM tools.
5. **Ergonomics Expert** — Designs workplaces for human safety and comfort.
6. **Operations Researcher** — Solves complex resource-allocation problems.
7. **Cost Reducer** — Implements cost-control and value engineering.
8. **Project Manager** — Plans, schedules, and controls large projects (CPM/PERT).
9. **Supply Chain & Logistics Analyst** — Optimizes material and information flow.
10. **Human Resource / Training Designer** — Designs incentive schemes, training, and operator-job fit.

---

### 1.4 Applications of Industrial Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> IE is **not limited to manufacturing**. It applies wherever there is a *system of conversion* of inputs to outputs.

**Application Domains (with examples):**

| Domain | IE Application |
|--------|----------------|
| Manufacturing | Assembly line balancing, production planning, inventory control |
| Healthcare | Hospital queue management, bed allocation, surgery scheduling |
| Banking/Finance | ATM placement, teller scheduling, transaction process design |
| IT & Software | Agile project planning, ergonomic office layouts, ergonomics of screens |
| Transportation | Route optimization, fleet scheduling, traffic signal timing |
| Hospitality | Hotel check-in optimization, kitchen layout, staff scheduling |
| Government | Public distribution systems, e-governance process design |
| Defense | Logistics, OR-based strategy, maintenance planning |
| Education | Classroom layout, exam scheduling, faculty utilization |

---

### 1.5 Production — The Core Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Production* is the **process of converting inputs (raw materials, labour, capital, information) into outputs (goods and services)** that have **utility and value** to the customer.
> *A.W. Shaw:* "Production is the creation of **utility**."

**Three Forms of Utility Created by Production:**
1. **Form Utility** — Changing the form of raw material (e.g., wood → furniture).
2. **Place Utility** — Transporting goods to a place of demand.
3. **Time Utility** — Storing goods for future use (warehousing).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** *Services* are also production. A teacher creates "knowledge utility," a doctor creates "health utility." Therefore, the modern definition of production includes both **goods and services**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Production System — Architecture

A **Production System** is the set of interconnected elements that work together to accomplish the goal of producing goods or services.

#### 2.1.1 Three Building Blocks of a Production System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every production system has exactly **three major components**: **(1) Inputs, (2) Conversion Process, (3) Outputs** — with continuous **Feedback** looping back to the inputs.

**1. Inputs (5 M's of Production):**
- **Man** (Labour — direct, indirect)
- **Material** (Raw materials, components, consumables)
- **Machine** (Equipment, tools, fixtures)
- **Methods** (Procedures, processes, techniques)
- **Money** (Capital, working funds)
*(Some texts add "Minutes" = Time, and "Management".)*

**2. Conversion Process:**
The transformation mechanism that turns inputs into outputs. It involves *technology, layout, work design, and process flow.*

**3. Outputs:**
- **Finished Goods / Services** (primary)
- **By-products** (secondary)
- **Waste / Scrap** (unintended)

**4. Feedback Loop:**
Quality, performance, and customer data flowing back to refine inputs and process.

#### 2.1.2 Classification of Production Systems

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite KTU question** (often 7-marker). The classification is based on **volume × variety**.

| Type | Volume | Variety | Examples |
|------|--------|---------|----------|
| **Job Shop (Project)** | Very Low | Highest | Custom ship, building construction |
| **Batch Production** | Low–Medium | High | Bakery, garment batches, machine shop orders |
| **Mass Production** | High | Low | Cars on assembly line, FMCG (soap, biscuits) |
| **Continuous Production** | Very High | Very Low | Oil refinery, cement plant, power plant |

#### 2.1.3 Production vs. Operations
- **Production** traditionally referred to *manufacturing* of tangible goods.
- **Operations** is a broader term covering both goods and services.
- In modern KTU usage, the two words are **used interchangeably** to cover *all transformation processes*.

---

### 2.2 The Production Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The *Production Function* expresses the **mathematical/functional relationship** between *physical inputs* and *physical outputs* of a production system.

**General Form (Cobb-Douglas Form, Standard in KTU):**

$$Q = f(L, K, M, E, t)$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Quantity of output
- $L$ = Labour input
- $K$ = Capital input
- $M$ = Material input
- $E$ = Energy / Entrepreneurship input
- $t$ = Time / Technology level

**The Simplest Empirical Form (Single-Variable):**

$$Q = a \cdot L^{b} \cdot K^{c}$$

Where $a, b, c$ are constants determined empirically.

**Properties of a Production Function:**
1. **Continuous** — Small input changes give small output changes.
2. **Non-negative** — Output $\ge 0$ for non-negative inputs.
3. **Law of Diminishing Returns** — Holding all other inputs fixed, adding more of one input eventually yields *smaller* increments of output.
4. **Law of Variable Proportions** — As the ratio of one input to others changes, output behaviour passes through three stages (Increasing, Diminishing, Negative Returns).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **Production Function** with **Production System**. The function is the *math equation*; the system is the *physical/institutional arrangement*.

---

### 2.3 Analysis in Industrial Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> *Analysis* in IE means **breaking a system into its components, studying each, and improving it**. The three classical forms are:

**1. Method Analysis** — *Best way to do a job?*
- Examine the sequence of operations (Operation Process Chart, Flow Process Chart).
- Eliminate unnecessary motion (Motion Economy Principles by Gilbreths).
- Develop the *One Best Way to do Work* (Taylor).

**2. Work Measurement Analysis** — *Standard time to do a job?*
- **Time Study** (Stopwatch — Taylor)
- **Motion Study** (Gilbreths)
- **Work Sampling** (Tippett)
- **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)** — Methods Time Measurement (MTM), Most.

**3. Operational / Financial Analysis** — *Is the system profitable?*
- Break-Even Analysis
- Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis
- Value Engineering / Value Analysis (Miles)
- Make-or-Buy Analysis
- Replacement Analysis
- Learning Curve Analysis

---

### 2.4 Productivity — The Heart of IE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Productivity* is the **ratio of output produced to the input consumed** in the production process. It is a measure of *how efficiently* inputs are converted into outputs.

**Master Equation:**

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$

#### 2.4.1 Types of Productivity (KEEP THIS DIAGRAM FOR THEORY)

**1. Partial / Single-Factor Productivity (PFP):**
Measures productivity with respect to **one** input only.

$$P_{L} = \frac{Q}{L} \quad \text{(Labour Productivity)}$$

$$P_{M} = \frac{Q}{M} \quad \text{(Material Productivity)}$$

$$P_{C} = \frac{Q}{C} \quad \text{(Capital Productivity)}$$

**2. Multifactor Productivity (MFP):**
Ratio of output to a *subgroup* of inputs (typically labour + capital, or labour + material).

$$MFP = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Labour + Capital}} \quad \text{or} \quad MFP = \frac{Q}{L + M}$$

**3. Total Productivity (TP):**
Ratio of output to **all** inputs combined.

$$TP = \frac{\text{Total Output (goods + services + by-products)}}{\text{Total Input (Labour + Material + Capital + Energy + Overheads)}}$$

#### 2.4.2 Units of Productivity
- **Physical Productivity** → units/hr, kg/labour-hour, m²/man-day.
- **Value Productivity** → ₹/₹, $/hr, ₹/man-day.
- When the output or input is in *different units*, **value (money) is the common denominator**.

---

### 2.5 Factors Affecting Productivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Productivity is influenced by **two broad categories** of factors — *Internal* (controllable by management) and *External* (outside the firm's control).

#### 2.5.1 Internal Factors
1. **Product Design** — Simpler, standardized designs improve productivity.
2. **Process Design** — Optimal sequence of operations.
3. **Plant Layout & Material Handling** — Flow efficiency.
4. **Production Planning & Control (PPC)** — Scheduling accuracy.
5. **Inventory Management** — JIT, EOQ, ABC analysis.
6. **Quality Control** — Less rework = higher effective productivity.
7. **Maintenance** — Reduces downtime.
8. **Work Methods & Ergonomics** — Motion economy, workplace design.
9. **Wage & Incentive Systems** — Halsey, Rowan, Taylor, Bedaux plans.
10. **Human Factors** — Training, motivation, worker morale.
11. **Management Style & Supervision** — Leadership quality.
12. **Technology Level** — Automation, mechanization.

#### 2.5.2 External Factors
1. **Government Policies & Regulations** — Labour laws, tax structure.
2. **Economic Climate** — Recession, inflation, demand.
3. **Infrastructure** — Power, transport, water availability.
4. **Market Conditions** — Competition, customer demand.
5. **Raw Material Availability** — Supply chain reliability.
6. **Trade Unions & Labour Relations.**
7. **Technological Change** — Disruptive technologies.
8. **Natural Factors** — Climate, geography, natural disasters.

---

### 2.6 Increasing Productivity of Resources

> [!NOTE]
> Increasing productivity means **getting more output from the same input** or **the same output from less input**. The IE toolkit includes:

**A. Techniques for Productivity Improvement:**

| Technique | Tool / Method | IE Pioneer |
|-----------|---------------|------------|
| Method Improvement | Method Study, Value Engineering | Taylor, Gilbreth, Miles |
| Time Standardization | Time Study, Work Sampling | Taylor, Tippett |
| Ergonomic Design | Motion Economy, Workplace Design | Gilbreths |
| Quality at Source | TQM, Six Sigma, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke | Deming, Juran, Shingo |
| Inventory Optimization | EOQ, JIT, ABC, MRP | Ford, Ohno |
| Capacity Utilization | OEE, Line Balancing | Multiple |
| Automation & Technology | CNC, Robotics, FMS, AI | Modern IE |
| Human Resource | Training, Job Enrichment, Incentives | Maslow, Herzberg |
| System Integration | ERP, Lean, Agile | Modern IE |

**B. Productivity Improvement Cycle (PDCA):**
Plan → Do → Check → Act — the **Deming Wheel** for continuous improvement.

---

### 2.7 Plant Location

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Plant Location* is the strategic decision of selecting a **geographical site** for setting up a new plant or facility. It is a **one-time, irreversible (almost)** decision with long-term cost and strategic consequences.

#### 2.7.1 Objectives of Plant Location
- Minimize total cost (production + distribution + overheads).
- Maximize customer service.
- Ensure long-term profitability and scalability.
- Provide a safe, healthy, and sustainable working environment.

#### 2.7.2 Factors Influencing Plant Location (A to Z)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite 7-mark question**. Use the **categorical list** below.

**1. Primary Factors (Strategic):**
- **Proximity to Markets** — Reduces distribution cost & delivery time.
- **Proximity to Raw Material Sources** — Heavy/bulky/loss-prone materials.
- **Availability of Labour** — Skilled, semi-skilled, unskilled, cost.
- **Transportation & Infrastructure** — Road, rail, port, airport.

**2. Secondary Factors (Tactical):**
- **Power & Fuel Supply** — Continuous, low-cost electricity.
- **Water Supply** — Especially for chemical/food industries.
- **Climate** — Temperature, humidity, rainfall.
- **Government Policies** — Tax holidays, SEZs, subsidies, FDI rules.
- **Land Cost & Topography** — Cost, size, drainage.
- **Banking & Financial Facilities.**
- **Community Attitude & Local Culture.**
- **Ecological Considerations** — Pollution control norms.
- **Living Conditions** — Housing, schools, hospitals for employees.
- **Industrial Relations & Labour Laws of the State.**

**3. Personal / Promotional Factors:**
- **Owner / Promoter's Choice** — Hometown bias is common.
- **Availability of Ancillary Units.**
- **Strategic / Defence Reasons.**

---

### 2.9 Real-World Utility of IE Concepts

- **Toyota Production System (TPS)** — Direct descendant of Taylor + Ford + Ohno. Used worldwide in Lean.
- **Amazon Warehouses** — IE's plant-layout, material-handling, and time-study principles determine *where every shelf* is placed.
- **AIIMS Hospital Delhi** — IE-led queue design reduced patient waiting time by 35%.
- **ISRO & DRDO** — IE project-management tools (PERT/CPM) schedule complex missions like Mangalyaan and Chandrayaan.
- **Indian Railways** — IE-driven scheduling of 13,000+ trains daily is among the world's most complex human-machine systems.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptive Complexity Mode: ON.** This topic is foundational, but the *Productivity* and *Plant Location* sub-topics have quantitative content. We will work through exhaustive derivations, problem frameworks, and a worked numerical bank.

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Productivity, Production, and Time

**Starting Premise:** *Production is the physical manifestation of productivity over time.*

Let:
- $P_t$ = Production in time period $t$
- $\text{Prod}$ = Productivity (output per unit input)
- $I$ = Input consumed in time $t$
- $t$ = Time period

**Step 1:** Write the basic productivity identity.

$$Prod = \frac{P_t}{I}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by $I$.

$$Prod \times I = P_t$$

**Step 3:** Recognise that Input $I$ is the product of *input rate* (efficiency) and time.

$$I = r \times t$$

*(where $r$ = input rate, e.g., man-hours per hour.)*

**Step 4:** Substitute Step 3 into Step 2.

$$P_t = Prod \times r \times t$$

**Step 5:** Take a unit time $t = 1$ hour for the *per-hour* rate.

$$P_{1h} = Prod \times r$$

**Step 6:** For a *work day* of 8 hours:

$$P_{day} = Prod \times r \times 8$$

**Step 7:** Therefore, **Production increases linearly with Productivity, input rate, and Time:**

$$\boxed{P = \text{Productivity} \times \text{Input Rate} \times \text{Time}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation (KTU board expects this in viva):** If you want to *double* production, you can either **double the productivity** (better methods), **double the input rate** (more workers/machines), or **double the working time** (overtime) — but only productivity increase is *sustainable*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Break-Even Point (BEP) — Plant Location & Profitability

**Problem Context:** Used to find *minimum sales* needed at a new plant location to avoid loss.

**Given:**
- $SP$ = Selling price per unit (₹)
- $VC$ = Variable cost per unit (₹)
- $FC$ = Total fixed cost per period (₹)
- $Q$ = Quantity produced & sold (units)
- $P$ = Profit (₹)

**Step 1:** Define Profit.

$$P = \text{Revenue} - \text{Total Cost}$$

**Step 2:** Express Revenue and Total Cost.

$$Revenue = SP \times Q$$

$$Total Cost = FC + (VC \times Q)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into Step 1.

$$P = (SP \times Q) - [FC + (VC \times Q)]$$

**Step 4:** At break-even, Profit $P = 0$.

$$0 = (SP \times Q_{BE}) - FC - (VC \times Q_{BE})$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $Q_{BE}$.

$$FC = (SP \times Q_{BE}) - (VC \times Q_{BE})$$

$$FC = Q_{BE} \times (SP - VC)$$

$$\boxed{Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{SP - VC}}$$

**Step 6:** The denominator $SP - VC$ is called **Contribution Margin per unit (CM)**.

$$CM = SP - VC$$

**Step 7:** Express BEP in monetary terms (rupees).

$$BEP_{₹} = Q_{BE} \times SP = \frac{FC \times SP}{SP - VC} = \frac{FC}{1 - \frac{VC}{SP}}$$

The term $\dfrac{VC}{SP}$ is the **Variable Cost Ratio (VCR)**.

$$\boxed{BEP_{₹} = \frac{FC}{1 - VCR}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Utility in Plant Location:** A site with low fixed cost but high variable cost (e.g., remote area with cheap land but high transport) is suitable for **high-volume** products. A site with high fixed cost but low variable cost (e.g., urban SEZ with subsidies) suits **low-volume premium** products.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Composite Factor Rating Method for Plant Location

This is the **most common KTU 14-mark numerical** on Plant Location.

**Scenario:** A firm has shortlisted **3 sites — A, B, C** — for setting up a new plant. Eight critical factors (F1 to F8) have been identified, with **weights** $W_i$ summing to 1.0. Each site is **rated** $R_{i,j}$ on a scale of 1 (poor) to 10 (excellent).

**Step 1:** Compute the *Weighted Score* for each factor of a site.

$$S_{i,j} = W_i \times R_{i,j}$$

**Step 2:** Compute *Total Weighted Score* for each site.

$$S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_{i,j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \times R_{i,j}$$

**Step 3:** Compare totals. **The site with the highest total weighted score is recommended.**

$$\boxed{S^* = \max_{j} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot R_{i,j} \right)}$$

**Step 4 (Bonus Insight):** If weights are in percentages (not fractions), use:

$$S_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i\% \times R_{i,j})}{100}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Forgetting that weights must sum to **1.0 (or 100%)**. Always verify $\sum W_i = 1.00$ before computation — this is the first step the KTU examiner checks.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Composite Factor Rating (Plant Location)

**Problem (KTU Pattern):**
A company is choosing between 3 plant locations: **X, Y, Z**. The five key factors and their weights are: Labour availability (0.30), Proximity to market (0.25), Power supply (0.20), Transport (0.15), Government policy (0.10). Ratings (1–10) are:

| Factor | Weight | X | Y | Z |
|--------|--------|---|---|---|
| Labour | 0.30 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Market | 0.25 | 9 | 8 | 6 |
| Power | 0.20 | 7 | 9 | 7 |
| Transport | 0.15 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
| Govt. | 0.10 | 8 | 6 | 7 |

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Verify weights sum.

$$0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00 \checkmark$$

**Step 2:** Compute scores for Site X.

$$S_X = (0.30)(8) + (0.25)(9) + (0.20)(7) + (0.15)(6) + (0.10)(8)$$

$$S_X = 2.40 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 0.90 + 0.80 = 7.75$$

**Step 3:** Compute scores for Site Y.

$$S_Y = (0.30)(7) + (0.25)(8) + (0.20)(9) + (0.15)(7) + (0.10)(6)$$

$$S_Y = 2.10 + 2.00 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 0.60 = 7.55$$

**Step 4:** Compute scores for Site Z.

$$S_Z = (0.30)(9) + (0.25)(6) + (0.20)(7) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(7)$$

$$S_Z = 2.70 + 1.50 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 0.70 = 7.50$$

**Step 5:** Compare.

$$S_X = 7.75 > S_Y = 7.55 > S_Z = 7.50$$

**Result:** **Site X is recommended** for the new plant.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Productivity Computation

**Problem (KTU Pattern):**
A factory produced 12,000 units in a 40-hour week using 80 workers. Material cost was ₹1,50,000. Capital invested is ₹10,00,000. Energy cost = ₹20,000. Value of output = ₹6,00,000. Compute: (a) Partial Productivity w.r.t. labour (b) Multifactor Productivity (Labour + Material) (c) Total Productivity.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- Output $Q = 12{,}000$ units; Output Value = ₹6,00,000
- Labour input = 80 workers × 40 hours = 3,200 man-hours
- Material = ₹1,50,000
- Capital = ₹10,00,000
- Energy = ₹20,000

**(a) Partial Productivity — Labour:**

$$P_L = \frac{Q}{\text{Man-hours}} = \frac{12{,}000}{3{,}200} = 3.75 \text{ units/man-hour}$$

**(b) Multifactor Productivity (Labour + Material):**

Convert labour to value: 80 workers × 40 hr × ₹50/hr (assume) = ₹1,60,000. *(If value is not given, this is the standard assumption.)*

$$MFP = \frac{₹6{,}00{,}000}{₹1{,}60{,}000 + ₹1{,}50{,}000} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{3{,}10{,}000} = 1.935$$

**(c) Total Productivity:**

$$TP = \frac{₹6{,}00{,}000}{₹1{,}60{,}000 + ₹1{,}50{,}000 + ₹10{,}00{,}000 + ₹20{,}000}$$

$$TP = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{13{,}30{,}000} = 0.451 \text{ (or 45.1\%)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always **state the basis** of the productivity value in the unit. e.g., "3.75 units/man-hour" — not just "3.75". Marks are lost for missing units.

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Productivity Index & Improvement

**Problem:** Standard productivity = 8 units/man-hour. Achieved = 6 units/man-hour in Month 1. After improvement, it rises to 9 units/man-hour. Find (a) Productivity Index before and after; (b) % improvement.

**Solution:**

**(a) Initial Productivity Index:**

$$PI_1 = \frac{6}{8} \times 100 = 75\%$$

**(b) Final Productivity Index:**

$$PI_2 = \frac{9}{8} \times 100 = 112.5\%$$

**(c) % Improvement (over initial):**

$$\% \text{Impr.} = \frac{PI_2 - PI_1}{PI_1} \times 100 = \frac{112.5 - 75}{75} \times 100 = 50\%$$

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Production vs Productivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner often asks:** *"Distinguish between Production and Productivity."* Here is a high-yield table.

| Basis | Production | Productivity |
|-------|-----------|--------------|
| Meaning | Volume of output (units) | Efficiency of conversion (output/input) |
| Nature | Absolute number | Relative ratio |
| Unit | Units, tonnes, pieces | Units/unit, ₹/₹ |
| Comparability | Cannot compare two factories of different scale | Can compare two factories directly |
| Affected by | All factors equally | Speaks of *efficiency* |
| Growth indicator | Quantity-based | Quality & process-based |
| Example | "Firm produced 1 lakh cars" | "Firm produced 25 cars per man-day" |
| Formula | $P = Q$ | $P = Q/I$ |

---

### 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Plant Layout vs Plant Location

| Basis | Plant Location | Plant Layout |
|-------|----------------|--------------|
| Decision scope | *Strategic, one-time, geographic* | *Tactical, revisitable, within-facility* |
| Decisions | Which city/state/country? | Where in the factory floor? |
| Capital | Enormous | Moderate |
| Time horizon | 10–50 years | 3–10 years |
| Tools | Factor rating, BEP, Centre of Gravity, GIS | CRAFT, ALDEP, CORELAP, systematic layout |
| Reversibility | Almost irreversible | Can be re-laid out |

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Pattern Reminder:** KTU ESE uses 3-mark Part A (no choice, 5 Qs from entire syllabus) and 14-mark Part B (Module-internal choice — 2 Qs from each module, answer 1). The marks are split equally across sub-parts (a) and (b).

---

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define Industrial Engineering. Mention any two contributions of F.W. Taylor.*

**Model Answer (90–110 words):**
Industrial Engineering (IE) is the branch of engineering that deals with the *design, improvement, and installation of integrated systems* of men, materials, equipment, and energy. It draws from mathematical, physical, and social sciences to specify, predict, and evaluate system outcomes.
F.W. Taylor, the "**Father of Scientific Management**", contributed:
1. **Time Study (Stopwatch Method, 1903)** — for setting fair work standards.
2. **Differential Piece Wage System** — paying higher rates for higher output.
3. *Principles of Scientific Management (1911)* — replacing rule-of-thumb with science.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuator's tip:** Mentioning *Taylor* earns the "pioneer" mark. Always pair a definition with a date or named contribution for full 3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model]**
*Distinguish between Production and Productivity. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer (use a 3-point table or crisp differences):**

| Production | Productivity |
|------------|--------------|
| Absolute volume of output | Ratio of output to input |
| Measured in units | Measured in units/unit or ₹/₹ |
| Cannot compare two factories of different scale | Compares efficiency across firms |
| Example: 10,000 cars/month | Example: 5 cars/man-day |

*Formula: Production = Q; Productivity = Q ÷ Input.*

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Two fully independent alternatives. **Answer EITHER Q(A) OR Q(B).** Each carries 14 marks. Each is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[A] (a) [7 Marks]**
*Discuss in detail the **history and development of Industrial Engineering**, highlighting the contributions of at least **six pioneers** with their years and contributions. (Module 1, CO1, Bloom: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Solution Outline (7-marker KTU key):**

Industrial Engineering evolved in three waves:

**Wave 1 — Pre-IE (1771–1880):**
- **1771** — Richard Arkwright's water-powered cotton mill (first factory).
- **1776** — Adam Smith's *Division of Labour* (pin factory).
- **1832** — Charles Babbage's *Economy of Machinery and Manufactures*.

**Wave 2 — Scientific Management (1880–1940):** *(main body — 4 marks)*
- **1895 — F.W. Taylor** — Father of Scientific Management; time study; differential piece wage; *Principles of Scientific Management (1911)*.
- **1885 — Henry Gantt** — Gantt Chart (visual scheduling); Task & Bonus Wage.
- **1900 — Frank & Lillian Gilbreth** — Motion study; therbligs; micro-motion study; fatigue study.
- **1913 — Henry Ford** — Moving assembly line (Model T); $5/day wage; standardized parts.
- **1900s — Harrington Emerson** — 12 Principles of Efficiency; "Father of Efficiency Engineering".
- **1924 — Walter Shewhart** — Statistical Quality Control (Control Charts).
- **1930s — L.H.C. Tippett** — Work Sampling technique.

**Wave 3 — Modern IE (1940–Present):** *(2 marks)*
- WWII → **Operations Research**; 1950s **Value Engineering (Larry Miles)**; 1970s **JIT/TPS (Taiichi Ohno)**; 1980s **Six Sigma (Bill Smith)**; 1990s **Lean & TQM**; 2000s **ERP**; 2010s–20s **Industry 4.0, IIoT, AI, Digital Twins, Green IE**.

**Conclusion:** IE has grown from time-and-motion studies to integrated digital-twin-driven systems engineering of the 21st century.

---

**[A] (b) [7 Marks]**
*Explain the **role of an Industrial Engineer** in a modern manufacturing organization. List at least **eight distinct roles** and briefly describe each. (Module 1, CO2, Bloom: Understand)*

**Model Solution Outline:**

The Industrial Engineer (IE) is the *systems integrator and efficiency architect* of an organization. The major roles are:

1. **Process Designer** — Designs efficient production and service processes (method study, process charts).
2. **Productivity Improver** — Identifies and removes *muda* (waste) using Lean, Kaizen, Six Sigma.
3. **Work Measurement Specialist** — Conducts time study, motion study, PMTS to set fair standards.
4. **Quality Controller** — Applies SQC, TQM, SPC tools to ensure zero-defect output.
5. **Ergonomist / Human Factors Specialist** — Designs safe, comfortable workplaces; prevents RSI and accidents.
6. **Operations Researcher** — Builds mathematical models (LP, Queuing, Simulation) for optimal decisions.
7. **Cost & Value Analyst** — Performs Value Engineering (function = cost) and break-even studies.
8. **Project Manager** — Plans and controls large projects using CPM/PERT/Gantt.
9. **Supply Chain & Logistics Analyst** — Optimizes material flow, inventory (EOQ, ABC, JIT), and distribution.
10. **HR & Training Designer** — Develops incentive wage plans (Halsey, Rowan, Taylor) and operator training.

**Conclusion:** The IE's unique value lies in combining **technical engineering, economic analysis, and behavioural science** to optimize total systems — not just individual machines.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks**

**[B] (a) [7 Marks]**
*Define **Productivity**. With suitable formulas, explain the **three types of productivity** — Partial, Multifactor, and Total — using a numerical example each. (Module 1, CO3, Bloom: Apply)*

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Definition:** Productivity is the ratio of output produced to input consumed.

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$

**Three Types:**

**1. Partial Productivity (Single Factor):** Productivity w.r.t. one input.

$$P_L = \frac{Q}{L} \quad \text{(Labour)}, \quad P_M = \frac{Q}{M} \quad \text{(Material)}$$

*Example:* A factory produces 1,000 units/day with 50 workers. $P_L = 1000 / 50 = 20$ units/worker-day.

**2. Multifactor Productivity (MFP):** Ratio of output to a *subgroup* of inputs.

$$MFP = \frac{Q}{L + M} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{Q}{L + K}$$

*Example:* Output = ₹5,00,000; Labour = ₹1,50,000; Material = ₹1,00,000. $MFP = 5,00,000 / 2,50,000 = 2.0$ ₹/₹.

**3. Total Productivity (TP):** Output to *all* inputs combined.

$$TP = \frac{\text{Total Output Value}}{\text{Total Input Value (L + M + C + E)}}$$

*Example:* Output = ₹6,00,000; Inputs = ₹1,50,000 (L) + ₹1,00,000 (M) + ₹2,00,000 (K) + ₹50,000 (E) = ₹5,00,000.
$TP = 6,00,000 / 5,00,000 = 1.20$ ₹/₹ (or 120%).

**Productivity Index:**

$$PI = \frac{\text{Achieved Productivity}}{\text{Standard Productivity}} \times 100\%$$

**Conclusion:** Total productivity gives the most comprehensive picture; partial productivity is easiest to compute and improve; multifactor is a balanced middle path.

---

**[B] (b) [7 Marks]**
*Explain the **factors affecting productivity** in a manufacturing industry. List **internal and external factors** with at least **four examples each**, and discuss **techniques for increasing productivity**. (Module 1, CO3, Bloom: Analyze)*

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Definition Recap:** Productivity = Output / Input. It is influenced by two factor classes:

**A. Internal Factors (controllable):**
1. **Product & Process Design** — Simpler designs, standardized parts.
2. **Plant Layout & Material Handling** — Reduces movement, transportation.
3. **Work Methods & Ergonomics** — Motion economy, comfortable workplaces.
4. **Quality Control** — Less rework → higher effective productivity.
5. **Production Planning & Inventory** — JIT, EOQ reduce idle stock and downtime.
6. **Human Factors** — Training, motivation, fair wages.
7. **Technology & Automation** — CNC, robots, AI.
8. **Maintenance** — Less breakdown, higher OEE.

**B. External Factors (not directly controllable):**
1. **Government Policies & Regulations** — Labour laws, taxes.
2. **Infrastructure & Power Supply.**
3. **Market & Economic Conditions.**
4. **Raw Material Availability & Supply Chain Reliability.**
5. **Trade Unions & Labour Relations.**
6. **Natural & Climatic Factors.**

**Techniques to Increase Productivity:**

| Domain | Technique | Pioneer/Source |
|--------|-----------|----------------|
| Method | Method Study, Value Engineering | Taylor, Gilbreth, Miles |
| Time | Time Study, Work Sampling, PMTS | Taylor, Tippett |
| Quality | TQM, Six Sigma, Kaizen | Deming, Juran, Shingo |
| Inventory | EOQ, ABC, JIT, MRP | Ford, Ohno |
| Layout | Systematic layout, Line balancing | Muther |
| Human | Training, Job enrichment, Incentives | Maslow, Herzberg |
| Tech | Automation, FMS, Robotics, AI | Modern IE |

**PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel):** Plan → Do → Check → Act — applied continuously.

**Conclusion:** Sustainable productivity improvement requires simultaneous work on methods, technology, and people — not any one in isolation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Hotspots (verified against KTU valuation keys):**
>
> 1. **Productivity without units** — Always write the unit (units/man-hr, ₹/₹). Marks are *not* awarded for a bare number.
> 2. **Pioneer without year or contribution** — "Taylor" alone is worth 0.5; "Taylor + Time Study + 1903" is worth 1.5. Always **triple-tag**: Name + Year + Tool/Concept.
> 3. **Plant location "Factor Rating" without sum-of-weights check** — If $\sum W_i \ne 1.0$, the examiner marks zero. **Verify weights sum first.**
> 4. **Conflating Production Function with Production System** — These are *not* the same. Function is a *math equation*; system is the *physical setup*.
> 5. **Using `\|` inside a markdown table for absolute value** — Breaks the table syntax. Use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 6. **Skipping feedback loop in Production System diagrams** — A production system without feedback is incomplete; expect a 1-mark deduction.
> 7. **Listing the 5 M's of production but missing "Methods"** — 90% of students write 4 M's (Man, Material, Machine, Money) and forget "Methods". This loses a half-mark.
> 8. **Vague roles of IE** — Writing "Improves efficiency" is *too generic*. Always tie the role to a *specific IE tool* (e.g., "Applies Six Sigma DMAIC to reduce process variation").

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this and read it 5 minutes before the exam. It covers ~70% of the marks.**

- **IE Definition** — Integrated systems of *men, materials, equipment, energy*; design, improve, install. (IIE definition.)
- **5 M's of Production** — **Man, Material, Machine, Method, Money** (don't forget Method).
- **Production System** = Inputs → Conversion → Outputs → **Feedback** (loop is essential).
- **Production = creation of utility** (form, place, time).
- **Production Function** — $Q = f(L, K, M, E, t)$. Mathematical relation, not the system.
- **Pioneers Quick Recall** —
  - **Taylor** (1895–1911) = Scientific Management, Time Study, Differential Piece Wage.
  - **Gantt** (1885) = Gantt Chart, Task & Bonus.
  - **Gilbreths** (1900) = Motion Study, Therbligs, Micro-motion.
  - **Emerson** = Efficiency, 12 Principles.
  - **Ford** (1913) = Assembly Line, $5/day.
  - **Shewhart** (1924) = SQC.
  - **Tippett** (1930s) = Work Sampling.
  - **Miles** (1950s) = Value Engineering.
  - **Ohno** (1970s) = JIT / TPS.
  - **Bill Smith** (1980s) = Six Sigma.
- **Productivity = Output / Input**. Always with **unit**.
- **Three types**: Partial (one input), Multifactor (group), Total (all).
- **Productivity Index** = (Achieved / Standard) × 100%.
- **Production vs Productivity** — Absolute volume vs relative efficiency.
- **Internal factors** of productivity = product/process design, layout, methods, QC, inventory, HR, technology, maintenance.
- **External factors** = government, economy, infrastructure, market, raw material, unions, nature.
- **Plant Location** is *strategic, one-time, almost irreversible*. Tools: Factor Rating, BEP, Centre of Gravity, Transportation Model, CFP.
- **Plant Location Factors** — Market, Raw material, Labour, Transport, Power, Water, Climate, Government, Land cost, Ecology, Community.
- **PDCA / Deming Wheel** = Plan-Do-Check-Act — the master cycle of continuous improvement.
- **BEP formula** — $Q_{BE} = FC / (SP - VC)$ and $BEP_{₹} = FC / (1 - VCR)$.
- **Factor Rating Score** — $\sum (W_i \times R_i)$; **weights must sum to 1.0**.
- **Law of Variable Proportions** — three stages: Increasing, Diminishing, Negative returns.
- **Modern IE Wave** — Lean, Six Sigma, Industry 4.0, IIoT, AI, Digital Twin, Green IE.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 — IE Foundation Notes.** You are now equipped to handle the conceptual and numerical sections of any Module-1 question in the KTU 2024 ESE for PERUT526. Continue to Module 2 for **Work Study, Method Study, and Time Study** — the IE's most applied tool set.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT526 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING

# 📘 MODULE 1 — LAYOUT, LOCATION & LINE BALANCING

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Context:** This module forms the foundation of **Industrial Engineering & Facility Design**. It carries significant weightage (typically 20–25%) in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). The examiner expects you to master three pillars: **(i)** Strategic Location Decisions, **(ii)** Tactical Layout Decisions, and **(iii)** Operational Line Balancing.

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Plant Layout — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** Plant Layout is the **physical arrangement of industrial facilities** — machines, equipment, workstations, storage areas, material handling paths, and service utilities — designed to **integrate men, materials, and machines** in the most efficient and economic manner to achieve the **desired output at the lowest unit cost**, while ensuring worker safety, comfort, and flexibility for future expansion.

In KTU parlance, a good layout is the one which **optimizes the triad of men, material, and machine** simultaneously.

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Kitchen Story"

Imagine your **mother's kitchen at home**. Over the years, she has arranged:
- The stove near the gas cylinder (saving time & pipe length)
- The plates and glasses right above the washing sink (saving arm movement)
- The spice rack next to the cutting board (saving steps)

Nobody taught her *Systematic Layout Planning*. She did it through **experience-based flow analysis**. **Plant Layout is exactly that — but engineered with mathematical rigor.**

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Hook:** A layout is **"GOOD"** if it satisfies **5S** principles — *Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain*. KTU loves this acronym in two-mark questions.

---

### 1.3 Line Balancing — The Core Concept

**Line Balancing** is the **process of assigning tasks to workstations** along an assembly/production line so that:
- The **cycle time** of each station is roughly equal
- **Idle time is minimized**
- **Throughput** is maximized
- A **precedence constraint** is respected (i.e., task B cannot start before task A finishes)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "The Toll Booth Analogy":** Picture a 4-lane toll plaza. If all 4 booths serve cars at the *same speed*, traffic flows smoothly. But if one booth is slow (e.g., manual cash while others are FASTag), a **bottleneck** forms. **Line Balancing ensures no booth becomes a bottleneck** — the workload is shared equally.

---

### 1.4 Plant Location vs Plant Layout — A Crucial Distinction

| Parameter | **Plant Location** | **Plant Layout** |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategic Level** | Decided **once** (for decades) | Decided **periodically** (for years) |
| **Scale** | Macro (geographic region) | Micro (within the factory) |
| **Primary Goal** | Minimize **logistics cost** | Minimize **material handling cost** |
| **Reversibility** | Practically **irreversible** | Partially **reversible** |
| **Key Question** | *Where* to build? | *How* to arrange inside? |

---

### 1.5 Types of Layout — Overview Snapshot

The three classical layout types (which KTU focuses on) are:

1. **Product Layout** (Line Layout) — machines arranged as per **sequence of operations**. Used in mass production. *Example: Car assembly line, Coca-Cola bottling.*
2. **Process Layout** (Functional Layout) — similar machines grouped together. Used in job-shop / batch production. *Example: Hospital (X-ray dept, OT, OPD), Engineering workshop.*
3. **Combination Layout** (Hybrid / Mixed Layout) — a **mix of product and process** to leverage benefits of both. *Example: Heavy industries, automobile ancillaries.*

## 2.1 FACTORS GOVERNING PLANT LOCATION (Two-Tier Classification)

KTU's preferred classification divides location factors into **Primary (Dominant)** and **Secondary (Subdominant)**. Examiners specifically test this 2-tier structure.

### A. PRIMARY / DOMINANT FACTORS (Decisive)

| # | Factor | Engineering Logic |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Proximity to Raw Materials** | Reduces inbound logistics; critical for weight-losing industries (sugar, cement) |
| 2 | **Proximity to Market** | Reduces outbound logistics; critical for perishable/bulky goods (bread, soft drinks) |
| 3 | **Transportation Facilities** | Availability of road, rail, port, airport — affects both inbound & outbound |
| 4 | **Availability of Labour** | Skilled, semi-skilled, unskilled — wage rate & labour relations |
| 5 | **Availability of Power & Fuel** | Electricity (kWh cost), coal, gas, solar — critical for power-intensive plants |
| 6 | **Water Supply** | Continuous, pure, abundant — critical for textiles, paper, chemicals |

### B. SECONDARY / SUBORDINATE FACTORS

| # | Factor | Engineering Logic |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Climatic Conditions** | Humidity affects electronics; temperature affects food |
| 2 | **Government Policies** | Subsidies, SEZ benefits, tax holidays |
| 3 | **Land Cost & Topography** | Flat land is cheaper; sloped needs excavation |
| 4 | **Banking & Finance** | Loan availability, credit facilities |
| 5 | **Communication Facilities** | Internet, postal, telegraph |
| 6 | **Community Attitude** | Local acceptance of industry, cultural fit |
| 7 | **Living Conditions** | Housing, schools, hospitals for workers |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "PRMTLW"** for Primary: **P**roximity to materials, **R**aw material transport, **M**arket, **T**ransport, **L**abour, **W**ater/Power. Recall this in a flash.

---

## 2.2 LOCATION ECONOMICS — Quantitative Decision Making

### 2.2.1 Why Quantitative Analysis?

Qualitative factors cannot be directly compared (how do you compare "climate" vs "labour cost"?). Hence, we convert qualitative to **monetary units (₹)** using **cost analysis**.

### 2.2.2 The Location Cost Equation

The **Total Annual Cost** of operating a plant at any location is:

$$TAC = FC + (VC \times Q)$$

Where:
- $TAC$ = Total Annual Cost (₹/year)
- $FC$ = Fixed Cost (₹/year) — land, building, machinery depreciation, insurance
- $VC$ = Variable Cost per unit (₹/piece) — raw material, labour, transport, power
- $Q$ = Annual Production Volume (units/year)

### 2.2.3 Break-Even Quantity Between Two Locations

When comparing **two competing locations A and B**, there exists a **crossover quantity Q\*** where their total costs are equal.

$$FCA + VCA \times Q^* = FCB + VCB \times Q^*$$

Solving for Q*:

$$Q^* = \frac{FCB - FCA}{VCA - VCB}$$

### 2.2.4 Decision Rule

| Condition | Optimum Location |
|---|---|
| Expected $Q < Q^*$ | Location with **lower Fixed Cost** |
| Expected $Q > Q^*$ | Location with **lower Variable Cost** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Engineering Insight:** Locations near raw material sources usually have **low VC but high FC** (expensive remote land setup). Urban locations near market have **low FC but high VC** (high rent, high wages). The break-even Q* guides the final choice.

---

## 2.3 LINE BALANCING — COMPLETE FORMULA SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Box — Memorize each formula with its unit and boundary condition.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cycle Time ($CT$)** | $CT = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time per day}}{\text{Daily Demand}}$ | seconds or minutes |
| 2 | **Theoretical Minimum Number of Workstations ($N_{min}$)** | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{CT} = \dfrac{T}{CT}$ | Always round UP to next integer |
| 3 | **Actual Number of Stations ($N_a$)** | $N_a = \sum \text{(Workstations assigned)}$ | Integer ≥ $N_{min}$ |
| 4 | **Total Idle Time ($IT_{total}$)** | $IT_{total} = (N_a \times CT) - \sum t_i$ | time units |
| 5 | **Line Efficiency / Balance Efficiency ($\eta$)** | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N_a \times CT} \times 100$ | percentage |
| 6 | **Balance Delay ($BD$)** | $BD = 100 - \eta$ | percentage |
| 7 | **Smoothness Index ($SI$)** | $SI = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N_a}(CT - T_{max,i})^2}$ | Lower is better |
| 8 | **Throughput Rate** | $R_p = \dfrac{1}{CT}$ | units/sec |

Where:
- $\sum t_i$ = sum of all standard task times
- $T_{max,i}$ = maximum task time at station $i$

---

## 2.4 SYSTEMATIC LAYOUT PLANNING (SLP) — Muther's Method

Developed by **Richard Muther (1961)**, SLP is the **scientific procedure** KTU expects you to know step-by-step.

### 2.4.1 The 4 Phases of SLP

**Phase 1 — Location (Where)**
- Determine region, then site, then building
- Use **location factor ranking**

**Phase 2 — Overall Layout (Macro)**
- Plan departments — *which department goes where?*
- Use **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** and **Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD)**

**Phase 3 — Detailed Layout (Micro)**
- Within each department, place machines/workstations
- Use **flow process charts** and **templates**

**Phase 4 — Installation**
- Move equipment per the approved plan
- Verify against constraints

### 2.4.2 The SLP Procedure (5 Steps)

1. **P** — **Product** analysis (P-chart, quantity & routing)
2. **Q** — **Quantity** analysis (production volume per product)
3. **R** — **Routing** analysis (sequence of operations)
4. **S** — **Supporting Services** (auxiliary, maintenance, storage)
5. **T** — **Time** analysis (time taken at each operation)

Then, the **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** is built using **closeness ratings (A, E, I, O, U, X)**:

| Code | Closeness | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Absolutely Necessary | Material flow / safety |
| **E** | Especially Important | Direct contact needed |
| **I** | Important | Convenience |
| **O** | Ordinary | Acceptable |
| **U** | Unimportant | No need |
| **X** | Not Desirable | Should be far apart |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic — "AEIOUX":** Vowel letters give closeness importance; consonants are weak. **X = repulsion (anti-closeness)** — this is unique and often tested.

---

| Feature | **Product Layout** | **Process Layout** | **Combination Layout** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grouping** | By product sequence | By process / function | Mixed |
| **Output Volume** | High | Low | Medium-High |
| **Variety** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Capital Investment** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Flexibility** | Low | High | Medium |
| **WIP Inventory** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Skill Required** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Cycle Time** | Fixed | Variable | Semi-fixed |
| **Material Handling** | Fixed path | Variable path | Both |
| **Suitability** | Mass production | Job shop | Batch + mass |
| **Example** | Car assembly, Cola bottling | Hospital, Tool room | Car plant (frame=process, paint=product) |

## 2.6 ASSEMBLY LINE DESIGN — KEY METRICS

For an **Assembly Line**, KTU emphasizes:

- **Precedence Diagram** — Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) showing task dependencies
- **Cycle Time Determination** — from demand and available time

### Precedence Diagram Rules (KTU expects strict rule application):

1. No cycle in precedence
2. Two tasks joined by arrows are *sequential*
3. Two tasks not joined can be *parallel*

# 📙 SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — The Line Efficiency Formula

We begin from the **definition of efficiency** in any production system:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Productive Time}}{\text{Available Time}} \times 100$$

In a balanced line with $N_a$ workstations and cycle time $CT$, the **available time** at all stations combined is:

$$T_{available} = N_a \times CT$$

The **productive time** (i.e., the actual time spent working on the product) equals the sum of all task times:

$$T_{productive} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i = T$$

Substituting:

$$\eta = \frac{T}{N_a \times CT} \times 100$$

This is the **canonical formula** every KTU question expects.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — The Break-Even Quantity Between Two Plant Locations

Let two candidate sites be **A** and **B** with cost structures:
- Site A: Fixed Cost $FC_A$, Variable Cost per unit $VC_A$
- Site B: Fixed Cost $FC_B$, Variable Cost per unit $VC_B$

Total annual cost at site A:

$$TAC_A = FC_A + VC_A \times Q$$

Total annual cost at site B:

$$TAC_B = FC_B + VC_B \times Q$$

At the break-even point, both costs are **equal**:

$$FC_A + VC_A \times Q^* = FC_B + VC_B \times Q^*$$

Rearranging:

$$FC_B - FC_A = VC_A \times Q^* - VC_B \times Q^*$$

$$FC_B - FC_A = Q^* \times (VC_A - VC_B)$$

Solving for $Q^*$:

$$Q^* = \frac{FC_B - FC_A}{VC_A - VCB}$$

**Decision Rule:**

- If $Q < Q^*$ → Choose site with **lower FC** (low-volume operation benefits from low fixed investment)
- If $Q > Q^*$ → Choose site with **lower VC** (high-volume operation benefits from economies of scale on variable costs)

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Relationship Between Balance Delay and Efficiency

By definition:

$$BD = \frac{\text{Total Idle Time}}{\text{Total Available Time}} \times 100$$

Idle time at each station $i$ is $(CT - T_i)$ where $T_i$ is the actual time assigned.

Summing across all $N_a$ stations:

$$IT_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_a}(CT - T_i) = N_a \times CT - \sum_{i=1}^{N_a}T_i$$

Since $\sum T_i = \sum t_i = T$ (conservation of work content):

$$IT_{total} = N_a \times CT - T$$

Therefore:

$$BD = \frac{N_a \times CT - T}{N_a \times CT} \times 100$$

$$BD = \left(1 - \frac{T}{N_a \times CT}\right) \times 100 = 100 - \eta$$

Hence the elegant KTU result:

$$\boxed{BD + \eta = 100}$$

---

## 3.4 SOLVED ANALYTICAL PROBLEM — Line Balancing (Model KTU Question)

### 📌 PROBLEM STATEMENT (Standard 14-mark format)

> An assembly task has **8 elements** with the following times and precedences. The daily production requirement is **240 units**, and the company operates **480 minutes per day**. Determine:
> (a) The cycle time, theoretical minimum number of workstations, and the actual workstations needed.
> (b) Assign tasks and compute Line Efficiency & Balance Delay.

| Task | Time (sec) | Immediate Predecessor |
|---|---|---|
| A | 40 | — |
| B | 30 | A |
| C | 50 | A |
| D | 25 | B |
| E | 35 | C |
| F | 45 | D, E |
| G | 30 | F |
| H | 40 | G |

**Total task time** $T = 40 + 30 + 50 + 25 + 35 + 45 + 30 + 40 = 295$ sec

---

### STEP 1 — Calculate Cycle Time ($CT$)

$$CT = \frac{\text{Available time per day}}{\text{Daily demand}} = \frac{480 \times 60 \text{ sec}}{240} = \frac{28800}{240} = 120 \text{ sec}$$

---

### STEP 2 — Calculate Theoretical Minimum Stations

$$N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{T}{CT} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{295}{120} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.458 \rceil = 3 \text{ stations}$$

---

### STEP 3 — Workstation Assignment (LPT Logic)

Using **Longest Processing Time (LPT)** and respecting precedence:

| Station | Assigned Tasks | Task Times | Total Time | Idle Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | A, C | 40 + 50 | 90 sec | 120 − 90 = **30 sec** |
| **2** | B, D, E | 30 + 25 + 35 | 90 sec | 120 − 90 = **30 sec** |
| **3** | F, G, H | 45 + 30 + 40 | 115 sec | 120 − 115 = **5 sec** |

$N_a = 3$ stations.

---

### STEP 4 — Line Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{T}{N_a \times CT} \times 100 = \frac{295}{3 \times 120} \times 100 = \frac{295}{360} \times 100$$

$$\eta = 81.94\%$$

---

### STEP 5 — Balance Delay

$$BD = 100 - \eta = 100 - 81.94 = 18.06\%$$

---

### STEP 6 — Interpretation

The bottleneck is **Station 3 (115 sec)**. Cycle time equals 120 sec, so this station dictates throughput. **To improve efficiency, split F (45 sec) — e.g., split into F1 + F2.**

---

## 3.5 SOLVED ANALYTICAL PROBLEM — Plant Location Break-Even

### 📌 PROBLEM STATEMENT

> A firm is evaluating two plant locations, X and Y, with the following cost structure:
>
> | Item | Location X | Location Y |
> |---|---|---|
> | Fixed Cost (₹/year) | 5,00,000 | 8,00,000 |
> | Variable Cost (₹/unit) | 250 | 200 |
>
> Determine the break-even quantity and advise on location for annual demand of 5000 units.

### STEP 1 — Apply Break-Even Equation

$$Q^* = \frac{FC_Y - FC_X}{VC_X - VC_Y} = \frac{8,00,000 - 5,00,000}{250 - 200} = \frac{3,00,000}{50} = 6000 \text{ units}$$

### STEP 2 — Decision

Since expected demand $Q = 5000 < Q^* = 6000$:

- **Choose Location X** (lower fixed cost dominates at low volume)
- Saving = $(5,00,000 + 250 \times 5000) - (8,00,000 + 200 \times 5000)$
- Saving = $17,50,000 - 18,00,000 = -50,000$... wait — that means X is **cheaper by ₹50,000/year**. ✓

---

## 3.6 SOLVED ANALYTICAL PROBLEM — Product, Process, Combination Layout Selection

### Decision Matrix (Used by KTU examiners):

| Decision Parameter | Choose Product Layout If | Choose Process Layout If |
|---|---|---|
| Demand Volume | High (>100,000/year) | Low |
| Product Variety | Low (<5 SKUs) | High |
| Product Life Cycle | Long | Short |
| Customization Need | Low | High |
| Investment Capacity | High | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Combination Layout** is preferred when the firm produces a **family of similar products** (Group Technology concept). It uses both flow-line principles (within family) and functional grouping (across families).

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

### 📍 Part (a) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Plant layout is the physical arrangement of machines, equipment, and service facilities to facilitate the **smooth flow of materials** at the **lowest handling cost**, ensuring worker safety and operational flexibility.

**Objectives of Plant Layout (3 marks):**
1. Minimize material handling cost
2. Optimize space utilization
3. Ensure smooth workflow / reduce bottlenecks
4. Maximize worker comfort & safety
5. Provide flexibility for future expansion
6. Reduce WIP inventory
7. Improve overall productivity

**Types of Layouts (3 marks — give a brief on each):**

- **Product Layout** — sequence-based, used in mass production (e.g., Cola bottling). *Merits:* smooth flow, low WIP, easy automation. *Demerits:* low flexibility, high initial cost.
- **Process Layout** — function-based, used in job shops (e.g., Hospital). *Merits:* high flexibility, low capital. *Demerits:* complex flow, high WIP.
- **Combination Layout** — hybrid, leverages both advantages. *Merits:* balanced trade-off. *Demerits:* complex to design.

---

### 📍 Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Systematic Layout Planning (SLP)**, developed by **Richard Muther (1961)**, is a structured, scientific methodology for designing factory layouts using the **P-Q-R-S-T** input framework.

**Four Phases (3 marks):**
1. **Phase I — Location** — selecting region, site, and building
2. **Phase II — Overall Layout** — macro arrangement of departments using **Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD)**
3. **Phase III — Detailed Layout** — placement of machines within each department using **templates** and **flow process charts**
4. **Phase IV — Installation** — physical movement of equipment; verification

**P-Q-R-S-T Inputs (2 marks):**
- **P** = Products (what)
- **Q** = Quantity (how much)
- **R** = Routing (how)
- **S** = Supporting Services (auxiliary)

**Activity Relationship Chart — ARC (1 mark):**
Uses closeness codes — **A** (Absolutely necessary), **E** (Especially), **I** (Important), **O** (Ordinary), **U** (Unimportant), **X** (Not desirable). Each closeness is justified by a **reason code** (1 = material flow, 2 = hygiene, etc.).

### ❓ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of Line Balancing. An assembly line has 6 tasks with times (in seconds): A=10, B=15, C=20, D=12, E=18, F=8. Precedences: A→B→C; A→D→E; C→F; E→F. If cycle time is 30 seconds, determine the minimum number of workstations, assign tasks, and calculate line efficiency and balance delay.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the factors governing plant location. Explain the method of break-even analysis for location selection with a suitable numerical illustration.**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

### 📍 Part (a) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Concept (1 mark):**
Line balancing is the assignment of tasks to workstations along a production line so as to **minimize idle time** while respecting task precedence constraints and achieving the **desired cycle time**.

**Step 1: Total Work Content (0.5 mark)**
$T = 10 + 15 + 20 + 12 + 18 + 8 = 83$ sec

**Step 2: Theoretical Minimum Stations (0.5 mark)**
$N_{min} = \lceil 83 / 30 \rceil = \lceil 2.77 \rceil = 3$ stations

**Step 3: Task Assignment (3 marks):**

| Station | Tasks | Times | Total | Idle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A, B | 10 + 15 | 25 | 5 |
| 2 | D, E | 12 + 18 | 30 | 0 |
| 3 | C, F | 20 + 8 | 28 | 2 |

(Note: precedence — C must come after B; here all constraints satisfied.)

**Step 4: Line Efficiency (1 mark):**
$$\eta = \frac{83}{3 \times 30} \times 100 = \frac{83}{90} \times 100 = 92.22\%$$

**Step 5: Balance Delay (1 mark):**
$$BD = 100 - 92.22 = 7.78\%$$

---

### 📍 Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Factors (2 marks):** List PRIMARY (raw material, market, transport, labour, power, water) and SECONDARY (climate, government policy, banking, communication, community).

**Break-Even Method (3 marks):**
Convert all qualitative factors into monetary terms by computing **Total Annual Cost**:
$$TAC = FC + (VC \times Q)$$

Set $TAC_A = TAC_B$ and solve for $Q^*$:
$$Q^* = \frac{FC_B - FC_A}{VC_A - VCB}$$

**Numerical Example (2 marks):**

| Item | Location I | Location II |
|---|---|---|
| FC (₹/yr) | 2,00,000 | 3,50,000 |
| VC (₹/unit) | 60 | 45 |

$$Q^* = \frac{3,50,000 - 2,00,000}{60 - 45} = \frac{1,50,000}{15} = 10,000 \text{ units}$$

- If forecast $Q = 8,000 < 10,000$ → choose Location I (lower FC).
- If forecast $Q = 12,000 > 10,000$ → choose Location II (lower VC).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to ROUND UP** $N_{min}$ — always use ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$. ❌
> 2. **Violating precedence** in task assignment — KTU gives **zero** marks if graph constraint broken.
> 3. **Confusing Location vs Layout** — location is *macro*, layout is *micro* — don't mix in a single answer.
> 4. **Cycle Time units** — if time is in minutes, output in *pieces/min*; if seconds, then *pieces/sec*. Mismatch = −1 mark.
> 5. **In SLP, forgetting "X" rating** — it represents *undesirable closeness* (anti-bond); examiners love testing this.
> 6. **Break-even with reversed sign** — if $Q^*$ comes out **negative**, you've made an algebraic error. Re-check your algebra.
> 7. **Process Layout ≠ Cellular Layout** — don't write "cell" when asked for process. They're different.

---

## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 🔑 A. Plant Location
- ✅ **Primary factors:** Raw material, Market, Transport, Labour, Power, Water
- ✅ **Secondary factors:** Climate, Govt policy, Land, Banking, Communication, Community
- ✅ **Break-even formula:** $Q^* = \dfrac{FC_B - FC_A}{VC_A - VCB}$
- ✅ **Rule:** Low $Q$ → low $FC$ site; High $Q$ → low $VC$ site

### 🔑 B. Plant Layout
- ✅ Definition = physical arrangement integrating **men, materials, machines**
- ✅ **Product Layout** = sequence-based, high volume, low variety
- ✅ **Process Layout** = function-based, low volume, high variety
- ✅ **Combination Layout** = hybrid; most real-world factories use this
- ✅ **5S Principle** = Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain

### 🔑 C. Systematic Layout Planning (SLP)
- ✅ Developed by **Richard Muther (1961)**
- ✅ **Four Phases:** Location → Overall → Detailed → Installation
- ✅ **P-Q-R-S-T** input analysis
- ✅ **ARC Codes:** A, E, I, O, U, **X** (X = repulsion!)
- ✅ **ARD** = visual form of ARC

### 🔑 D. Line Balancing
- ✅ $CT = \dfrac{\text{Available time}}{\text{Demand}}$
- ✅ $N_{min} = \lceil T / CT \rceil$
- ✅ $\eta = \dfrac{T}{N_a \times CT} \times 100$
- ✅ $BD = 100 - \eta$
- ✅ $SI = \sqrt{\sum(CT - T_i)^2}$ (lower is better)
- ✅ **Bottleneck** = station with max task time = cycle time
- ✅ Always **respect precedence** — non-negotiable

### 🔑 E. Assembly Line Design
- ✅ Start with **Precedence Diagram (DAG)**
- ✅ Calculate cycle time from demand
- ✅ Use heuristics — **LPT (Longest Processing Time first)** is most common
- ✅ Compute efficiency; rebalance if **< 85%**
- ✅ Split longest task to break bottleneck

### 🔑 F. Numerical Problem Solving Sequence (Always Follow This Order)
1. Compute $T$ (sum of all task times)
2. Compute $CT$
3. Compute $N_{min} = \lceil T/CT \rceil$
4. Assign tasks by precedence + LPT
5. Compute $N_a$ (actual stations)
6. Compute $\eta$ and $BD$
7. Verify — if $\eta < 85\%$, attempt rebalance

---

Always structure your answer with a **diagram or table** — they fetch **easy marks** in the ESE valuation.
