topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT526 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Line Balancing Methods - Computer Applications in Layout Designs - CORELAP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Line Balancing — Formal KTU Definition

**Assembly Line Balancing (ALB)** is the procedure of assigning tasks to workstations along an assembly line so as to achieve the desired output rate with the **minimum number of workstations** (or minimum cycle time), while observing the **precedence constraints** and ensuring that the total task time assigned to each workstation does not exceed the cycle time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Line balancing is the allocation of work elements among a series of workstations arranged in a sequence, such that the *precedence relationships are satisfied*, *cycle time constraints are met*, and the *workload is distributed as evenly as possible*.

The output of a balancing exercise is governed by three fundamental parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| **Cycle Time** | $C_t$ | Maximum time allowed at each workstation |
| **Number of Workstations** | $N$ | Minimum theoretical count needed |
| **Line Efficiency** | $\eta$ | Ratio of productive time to total available time |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a busy Indian restaurant kitchen preparing 50 thalis per hour. The head chef must decide: *who chops onions, who fries the masala, who plates the rice?* If one cook is idle while another is buried in work, the kitchen is *imbalanced*. **Line balancing is exactly this problem in industrial scale** — distributing the *workload (tasks)* across *workers (workstations)* so that no one is a bottleneck and no one is free, given that certain tasks *must* happen before others (you cannot plate before you cook).

## 1.3 Computer Applications in Layout Design

Manual layout planning is tedious and suboptimal. Computerized layout algorithms fall into two broad families:

1. **Construction-type algorithms** — Build the layout iteratively (CORELAP, ALDEP, CRAFT).
2. **Improvement-type algorithms** — Start with an initial layout and improve it (CRAFT — Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique).

> [!NOTE]
> **CORELAP** stands for **COmputerized RElationship LAyout Planning**. Developed by **Lee and Moore (1967)**, it uses the qualitative *Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)* as input and produces a block layout that maximizes the sum of closeness ratings between adjacent departments.

## 1.4 Why CORELAP Matters in KTU Context

KTU examiners frequently test:
- Computation of **Total Closeness Rating (TCR)**.
- Step-by-step department placement.
- Computation of **Total Closeness Value (TCV)** and **Relative Total Closeness (RTC)** for layout scoring.

These are *high-yield* marks. Master CORELAP and you secure a guaranteed 7–8 marks on any Module 1 question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Line Balancing — Precedence Diagrams

A precedence diagram is a **directed graph** where:

- **Nodes** represent work elements (tasks $i$).
- **Arcs** represent *must-come-before* relationships.
- Each node carries its task time $t_i$.

The **sum of all task times** is the *total work content*:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i = T$$

## 2.2 Cycle Time Derivation

For a required production rate $R$ (units/day) and available time $T_{avail}$:

$$C_t = \frac{T_{avail}}{R}$$

The **minimum number of workstations** (lower bound) is:

$$N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{\sum t_i}{C_t} \right\rceil = \left\lceil T \cdot \frac{R}{T_{avail}} \right\rceil$$

## 2.3 Line Balancing Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following four metrics are **universally tested in KTU examinations**. Memorize them as a single block.

### (a) Line Efficiency $\eta$

$$\eta = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{N \cdot C_t} \times 100\ \%$$

### (b) Balance Delay $BD$

$$BD = \frac{N \cdot C_t - \sum t_i}{N \cdot C_t} \times 100\ \% = 1 - \eta$$

### (c) Smoothness Index $SI$

$$SI = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{N} \left( C_t - T_k \right)^2}$$

where $T_k$ is the actual time assigned to workstation $k$. *Lower SI = better balance.*

### (d) Idle Time $I$

$$I = N \cdot C_t - \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$$

## 2.4 Ranked Positional Weight (RPW) Method — Helgeson-Birnie

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RPW is the most commonly tested line balancing heuristic in KTU papers.**

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. For each task $i$, compute its **positional weight** $PW_i$:
$$PW_i = t_i + \sum_{j \in S_i} t_j$$
where $S_i$ is the set of *all successors* (direct and indirect) of task $i$.

2. Rank tasks in **descending order of $PW$**.

3. Assign tasks to workstation 1 in PW order, subject to precedence and cycle time $C_t$.

4. When a task would exceed $C_t$, close the workstation and open a new one.

5. Continue until all tasks are assigned.

## 2.5 CORELAP — Theoretical Framework

> [!NOTE]
> CORELAP requires a *qualitative* input called the **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)**, which is constructed from the *Activity Relationship Diagram* using standard closeness codes.

### 2.5.1 Standard Closeness Codes (Lee & Moore, 1967)

| Code | Meaning | Numeric Rating |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **A** | Absolutely necessary | **6** |
| **E** | Especially important | **5** |
| **I** | Important | **4** |
| **O** | Ordinary closeness | **3** |
| **U** | Unimportant | **2** |
| **X** | Not desirable | **1** |

### 2.5.2 The CORELAP Algorithm

1. **Input** the ARC.
2. Compute **TCR (Total Closeness Rating)** for each department:
$$TCR_j = \sum_{k=1, k \neq j}^{m} c_{jk}$$
where $c_{jk}$ is the closeness rating between departments $j$ and $k$, and $m$ is the number of departments.

3. **Start placement**: place the department with the **highest TCR** in the center of the layout grid.

4. **Iteration step**: for each unplaced department, compute a *Placement Score* with the already-placed departments:
$$PS_j = \sum_{k \in Placed} c_{jk}$$

5. **Tie-breaking rule**: if a tie occurs, choose the department with the higher TCR.

6. Place the selected department in the empty grid cell that is **adjacent to the most important relationship** (i.e., next to the placed department with which it has the highest closeness rating). For ties, the corner position is preferred.

7. Repeat until all departments are placed.

### 2.5.3 Layout Scoring — TCV and RTC

After the layout is built, **score** the result:

**Total Closeness Value (TCV)** is computed *only* for **adjacent** (touching-side) department pairs:

$$TCV = \sum_{i,j \ \text{adjacent}} c_{ij} + \sum_{i,j \ \text{diagonal}} \frac{c_{ij}}{2}$$

**Maximum possible closeness sum (MPCS)** assuming ideal pairing:

$$MPCS = \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} k \cdot (\text{highest rating in column } k)$$

**Relative Total Closeness (RTC):**

$$RTC = \frac{TCV}{MPCS} \times 100\ \%$$

A higher RTC indicates a better-quality layout.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------|---------------|
| Cycle Time | $C_t = T_{avail} / R$ | minutes/unit |
| Min. Workstations | $N_{min} = \lceil \sum t_i / C_t \rceil$ | dimensionless |
| Line Efficiency | $\eta = \sum t_i / (N \cdot C_t) \times 100$ | percent |
| Balance Delay | $BD = 1 - \eta$ | decimal / percent |
| Smoothness Index | $SI = \sqrt{\sum_k (C_t - T_k)^2}$ | minutes |
| Positional Weight | $PW_i = t_i + \sum_{j \in S_i} t_j$ | minutes |
| Total Closeness Rating | $TCR_j = \sum_{k} c_{jk}$ | dimensionless (1–6 scale) |
| Placement Score | $PS_j = \sum_{k \in Placed} c_{jk}$ | dimensionless |
| Total Closeness Value | $TCV = \sum_{\text{adj}} c_{ij} + \sum_{\text{diag}} c_{ij}/2$ | dimensionless |
| Relative Total Closeness | $RTC = TCV / MPCS \times 100$ | percent |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Line balancing** is used in Toyota Production System, Maruti assembly lines, and Apple iPhone final assembly.
- **CORELAP** is used in hospital wing design, university department planning, and warehouse slotting. Modern tools like **Plant Simulation (Siemens)** and **FlexSim** are descendants of these classic algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — RPW Method on a 10-Element Assembly

**Problem Statement:**
A product has 10 work elements with precedence and times as follows. Available time per day = 480 minutes. Required output = 80 units/day. Use RPW method.

| Task | Time (min) | Immediate Predecessor |
|------|-----------|----------------------|
| A | 1.0 | — |
| B | 0.7 | — |
| C | 1.5 | A |
| D | 0.5 | A, B |
| E | 1.2 | C, D |
| F | 0.8 | D |
| G | 1.0 | E |
| H | 0.6 | E, F |
| I | 0.9 | G |
| J | 0.4 | H, I |

### Step 1 — Compute Cycle Time

$$C_t = \frac{T_{avail}}{R} = \frac{480}{80} = 6 \ \text{min/unit}$$

### Step 2 — Identify Successors

Successor set $S_i$ includes *all* direct and indirect successors.

| Task | Direct Successors | All Successors $S_i$ |
|------|------------------|----------------------|
| A | C, D | C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J |
| B | D | D, E, F, G, H, I, J |
| C | E | E, G, H, I, J |
| D | E, F | E, F, G, H, I, J |
| E | G, H | G, H, I, J |
| F | H | H, J |
| G | I | I, J |
| H | J | J |
| I | J | J |
| J | — | — |

### Step 3 — Compute Positional Weight $PW_i = t_i + \sum_{j \in S_i} t_j$

$$PW_A = 1.0 + (1.5+0.5+1.2+0.8+1.0+0.6+0.9+0.4) = 1.0 + 6.9 = 7.9$$

$$PW_B = 0.7 + (0.5+1.2+0.8+1.0+0.6+0.9+0.4) = 0.7 + 5.4 = 6.1$$

$$PW_C = 1.5 + (1.2+1.0+0.6+0.9+0.4) = 1.5 + 4.1 = 5.6$$

$$PW_D = 0.5 + (1.2+0.8+1.0+0.6+0.9+0.4) = 0.5 + 4.9 = 5.4$$

$$PW_E = 1.2 + (1.0+0.6+0.9+0.4) = 1.2 + 2.9 = 4.1$$

$$PW_F = 0.8 + (0.6+0.4) = 0.8 + 1.0 = 1.8$$

$$PW_G = 1.0 + (0.9+0.4) = 1.0 + 1.3 = 2.3$$

$$PW_H = 0.6 + 0.4 = 1.0$$

$$PW_I = 0.9 + 0.4 = 1.3$$

$$PW_J = 0.4 + 0 = 0.4$$

### Step 4 — Rank by $PW$ in Descending Order

| Rank | Task | $PW$ | Time $t_i$ |
|------|------|------|-----------|
| 1 | A | 7.9 | 1.0 |
| 2 | B | 6.1 | 0.7 |
| 3 | C | 5.6 | 1.5 |
| 4 | D | 5.4 | 0.5 |
| 5 | E | 4.1 | 1.2 |
| 6 | G | 2.3 | 1.0 |
| 7 | F | 1.8 | 0.8 |
| 8 | I | 1.3 | 0.9 |
| 9 | H | 1.0 | 0.6 |
| 10 | J | 0.4 | 0.4 |

### Step 5 — Assign Tasks to Workstations ($C_t = 6$ min)

**Workstation 1:**
- A (1.0) → cumulative = 1.0 ✓ (precedence: none)
- B (0.7) → cumulative = 1.7 ✓
- C (1.5) → cumulative = 3.2 ✓ (predecessor A done)
- D (0.5) → cumulative = 3.7 ✓ (predecessors A, B done)
- E (1.2) → cumulative = 4.9 ✓ (predecessors C, D done)
- G (1.0) → cumulative = 5.9 ✓ (predecessor E done)
- F (0.8) → cumulative = 6.7 ✗ exceeds $C_t$

So WS1 = {A, B, C, D, E, G} = 5.9 min, idle = 0.1 min

**Workstation 2:**
- F (0.8) → cumulative = 0.8 ✓
- I (0.9) → cumulative = 1.7 ✓ (G done)
- H (0.6) → cumulative = 2.3 ✓ (E, F done)
- J (0.4) → cumulative = 2.7 ✓ (H, I done)

WS2 = {F, I, H, J} = 2.7 min, idle = 3.3 min

### Step 6 — Compute Line Performance

$$\sum t_i = 1.0+0.7+1.5+0.5+1.2+0.8+1.0+0.6+0.9+0.4 = 8.6 \ \text{min}$$

$$N = 2$$

$$\eta = \frac{8.6}{2 \times 6} \times 100 = 71.67\ \%$$

$$BD = 1 - 0.7167 = 28.33\ \%$$

$$SI = \sqrt{(6-5.9)^2 + (6-2.7)^2} = \sqrt{0.01 + 10.89} = \sqrt{10.90} = 3.30 \ \text{min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The high balance delay here is a typical KTU exam lesson: **RPW is heuristic, not optimal**. More advanced methods (COMSOAL) can often do better.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — CORELAP Algorithm on a 6-Department Layout

**Problem Statement:**
A plant has 6 departments (1 to 6). The Activity Relationship Chart is:

| Dept Pair | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | — | A | E | I | O | U |
| 2 | A | — | A | E | I | O |
| 3 | E | A | — | A | E | I |
| 4 | I | E | A | — | A | E |
| 5 | O | I | E | A | — | A |
| 6 | U | O | I | E | A | — |

### Step 1 — Convert ARC to Numeric Ratings (using 6,5,4,3,2,1)

### Step 2 — Compute TCR for Each Department

$$TCR_1 = 6+5+4+3+2 = 20$$

$$TCR_2 = 6+6+5+4+3 = 24$$

$$TCR_3 = 5+6+6+5+4 = 26$$

$$TCR_4 = 4+5+6+6+5 = 26$$

$$TCR_5 = 3+4+5+6+6 = 24$$

$$TCR_6 = 2+3+4+5+6 = 20$$

### Step 3 — Pick Initial Department

Departments 3 and 4 tie at TCR = 26. Apply tie-breaker: choose the one with the *stronger* sum of *highest three* relationships. Dept 3's top 3 = 6+6+5 = 17; Dept 4's top 3 = 6+6+5 = 17. Truly tied — choose **Dept 3** (lower index by convention).

**Place Dept 3 at center of grid:**

```
.  .  .
.  3  .
.  .  .
```

### Step 4 — Compute Placement Score (PS) for Unplaced Departments

With only Dept 3 placed:

$$PS_1 = c_{13} = 5$$

$$PS_2 = c_{23} = 6$$

$$PS_4 = c_{34} = 6$$

$$PS_5 = c_{35} = 5$$

$$PS_6 = c_{36} = 4$$

**Highest PS tie:** Dept 2 and Dept 4 both at 6. **Choose Dept 4** (higher TCR = 26 vs 24).

**Place Dept 4 adjacent to Dept 3** (north, south, east, or west). Prefer corner-adjacency; place east:

```
.  .  .
.  3  4
.  .  .
```

### Step 5 — Next Iteration (Placed = {3, 4})

$$PS_1 = c_{13} + c_{14} = 5+4 = 9$$

$$PS_2 = c_{23} + c_{24} = 6+5 = 11$$

$$PS_5 = c_{35} + c_{45} = 5+6 = 11$$

$$PS_6 = c_{36} + c_{46} = 4+5 = 9$$

**Tie between Dept 2 and Dept 5** (both PS = 11). Tie-breaker = higher TCR → **Dept 2** (TCR = 24 > 24; actually equal, use index → Dept 2).

**Place Dept 2** adjacent to highest-relationship placed dept. Dept 2 has A (6) with Dept 3. Place west of Dept 3:

```
.  .  .
2  3  4
.  .  .
```

### Step 6 — Next Iteration (Placed = {2, 3, 4})

$$PS_1 = c_{12} + c_{13} + c_{14} = 6+5+4 = 15$$

$$PS_5 = c_{25} + c_{35} + c_{45} = 4+5+6 = 15$$

$$PS_6 = c_{26} + c_{36} + c_{46} = 3+4+5 = 12$$

**Tie between Dept 1 and Dept 5** (PS = 15). Tie-breaker = TCR → **Dept 5** (TCR = 24) > Dept 1 (TCR = 20). Choose **Dept 5**.

**Place Dept 5** next to Dept 4 (relationship = 6, A). Place south of Dept 4:

```
.  .  .
2  3  4
.  .  5
```

### Step 7 — Final Iterations

(Placed = {2, 3, 4, 5})

$$PS_1 = 6+5+4+3 = 18$$

$$PS_6 = 3+4+5+6 = 18$$

**Tie** — TCR tie-breaker → **Dept 1** (TCR = 20) and Dept 6 (TCR = 20). Use index → Dept 1.

**Place Dept 1** next to Dept 2 (A=6). Place north of Dept 2:

```
.  1  .
2  3  4
.  .  5
```

**Place Dept 6** in the only remaining slot. Adjacent to Dept 5 (A=6):

```
.  1  .
2  3  4
6  .  5
```

### Step 8 — Compute TCV

Adjacencies (sharing a side):

- (1, 2): 6
- (1, 3): 5
- (2, 3): 6
- (2, 6): 3
- (3, 4): 6
- (3, 5): wait — 3 is at center, 5 is at southeast. Diagonal only.
- (4, 5): 6
- (5, 6): 6
- (3, 6): 4 (not adjacent, dept 6 is southwest, dept 3 is center — diagonal)
- (1, 6): 2 (diagonal)

Wait, let me re-check the grid positions:

```
Row 1:  .  1  .
Row 2:  2  3  4
Row 3:  6  .  5
```

Adjacencies (side-sharing):
- 1–2: vertical (rows 1-2, col 2) ✓ → 6
- 1–3: horizontal (row 1, cols 1-2) ✓ → 5
- 2–3: horizontal (row 2, cols 1-2) ✓ → 6
- 2–6: vertical (rows 2-3, col 1) ✓ → 3
- 3–4: horizontal (row 2, cols 2-3) ✓ → 6
- 4–5: vertical (rows 2-3, col 3) ✓ → 6
- 5–6: horizontal (row 3, cols 1-3) ✓ → 6
- 3–5: vertical (rows 2-3, col 2) ✓ → 5
- 4–6: not adjacent (col 3 row 2 vs col 1 row 3) — diagonal
- 1–6: not adjacent — diagonal
- 1–4: not adjacent
- 1–5: not adjacent
- 2–4: not adjacent
- 2–5: not adjacent
- 3–6: not adjacent (diagonal)

**Diagonal pairs:**
- 4–6: 5 → contribution = 5/2 = 2.5
- 1–6: 2 → contribution = 2/2 = 1.0
- 1–4: 4 → contribution = 4/2 = 2.0
- 1–5: 3 → contribution = 3/2 = 1.5
- 2–4: 5 → contribution = 5/2 = 2.5
- 2–5: 4 → contribution = 4/2 = 2.0
- 3–6: 4 → contribution = 4/2 = 2.0

**Sum of adjacencies:**

$$TCV_{adj} = 6+5+6+3+6+6+6+5 = 43$$

**Sum of diagonals:**

$$TCV_{diag} = 2.5+1.0+2.0+1.5+2.5+2.0+2.0 = 13.5$$

$$TCV = 43 + 13.5 = 56.5$$

### Step 9 — Compute MPCS

The 6 departments have 15 paired relationships. Sort ratings in descending order: 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 5, 5, 5, 5, 5, 4, 4, 4, 3.

For MPCS, the ideal layout pairs the highest-rated relationship first. There are 6 ratings of value 6, then 5 ratings of 5, then 3 ratings of 4, then 1 rating of 3.

**Standard MPCS formula** uses Lee-Moore table:

$$MPCS = 6(6) + 5(5) + 4(4) + 3(3) + 2(2) + 1(1)$$

Wait — that's not quite right. The Lee-Moore MPCS for 6 departments:

$$MPCS = \sum_{k=1}^{5} k \cdot r_k$$

where $r_k$ is the *k-th highest* rating. With sorted ratings 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 5, 5, 5, 5, 5, 4, 4, 4, 3:

- $k=1$: 1 × 6 = 6
- $k=2$: 2 × 6 = 12
- $k=3$: 3 × 6 = 18
- $k=4$: 4 × 6 = 24
- $k=5$: 5 × 6 = 30

$$MPCS = 6+12+18+24+30 = 90$$

### Step 10 — Compute RTC

$$RTC = \frac{TCV}{MPCS} \times 100 = \frac{56.5}{90} \times 100 = 62.78\ \%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An RTC above **60%** is considered a *good* CORELAP layout. Values above **70%** are excellent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RPW Method — Algorithmic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Input tasks, times, precedence] --> nodeB[Build predecessor list for each task]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Find all successors of each task]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute PW = t_i + sum of successor times]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Rank tasks by descending PW]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Open Workstation 1]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Pick highest PW task with satisfied precedence}
    nodeG --> nodeH{Assigned time + t_i less than C_t?}
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[Assign task to workstation]
    nodeI --> nodeG
    nodeH -->|No| nodeJ[Close workstation, open next]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{All tasks assigned?}
    nodeK -->|No| nodeG
    nodeK -->|Yes| nodeL[Compute efficiency and SI]
    nodeL --> nodeM[End]
```

## 4.2 CORELAP Algorithm — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Assembly Line Balancing". List any two performance measures used to evaluate a line balancing solution.*

**Model Answer:**

Assembly Line Balancing is the procedure of assigning work elements to workstations along a production line, subject to precedence constraints and cycle time limits, to achieve the required output rate with the minimum number of workstations (or the most even workload distribution).

Two performance measures:

1. **Line Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N \cdot C_t} \times 100\%$ — measures how much productive time is utilized.
2. **Balance Delay** $BD = 1 - \eta$ — measures the proportion of idle time relative to total available time.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** *What is the meaning of "TCR" in CORELAP? How is the department with the highest TCR treated in the algorithm?*

**Model Answer:**

**TCR (Total Closeness Rating)** of a department $j$ is the sum of closeness ratings between department $j$ and *every* other department in the facility:

$$TCR_j = \sum_{k=1, k \neq j}^{m} c_{jk}$$

The department with the **highest TCR** is treated as the *seed* (starting point) of the layout. It is placed at the **center of the grid** because it has the strongest overall relationship with all other departments, and central placement minimizes the average distance to the rest of the plant.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *The following 8 tasks must be assigned to workstations on an assembly line. The cycle time is 12 minutes. Use the Ranked Positional Weight (RPW) method to balance the line. Compute line efficiency, balance delay, and smoothness index.*

| Task | Time (min) | Predecessors |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| A | 3 | — |
| B | 4 | — |
| C | 2 | A |
| D | 3 | A |
| E | 2 | B, C |
| F | 4 | D |
| G | 3 | E |
| H | 5 | F, G |

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Identify successors**

- $S_A = \{C, D, E, F, G, H\}$
- $S_B = \{E, G, H\}$
- $S_C = \{E, G, H\}$
- $S_D = \{F, H\}$
- $S_E = \{G, H\}$
- $S_F = \{H\}$
- $S_G = \{H\}$
- $S_H = \{\}$

**Step 2: Compute Positional Weights**

$$PW_A = 3 + (2+3+2+4+3+5) = 3+19 = 22$$

$$PW_B = 4 + (2+3+5) = 4+10 = 14$$

$$PW_C = 2 + (2+3+5) = 2+10 = 12$$

$$PW_D = 3 + (4+5) = 3+9 = 12$$

$$PW_E = 2 + (3+5) = 2+8 = 10$$

$$PW_F = 4 + 5 = 9$$

$$PW_G = 3 + 5 = 8$$

$$PW_H = 5 + 0 = 5$$

**Step 3: Ranked order: A(22), B(14), C(12), D(12), E(10), F(9), G(8), H(5)**

**Step 4: Assign to workstations ($C_t = 12$)**

- **WS1**: A(3) → B(4) → C(2) → D(3) → cumulative = 12 ✓ (WS1 closed; idle = 0)
- **WS2**: E(2) → F(4) → G(3) → cumulative = 9 → H(5)? cumulative = 14 ✗
- **WS2**: {E, F, G} = 9, idle = 3
- **WS3**: H(5), idle = 7

**Step 5: Performance**

$$\sum t_i = 3+4+2+3+2+4+3+5 = 26 \ \text{min}$$

$$N = 3, \quad C_t = 12$$

$$\eta = \frac{26}{3 \times 12} \times 100 = 72.22\ \%$$

$$BD = 1 - 0.7222 = 27.78\ \%$$

$$SI = \sqrt{(12-12)^2 + (12-9)^2 + (12-5)^2} = \sqrt{0+9+49} = \sqrt{58} = 7.62 \ \text{min}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to include *all* indirect successors when computing $PW$. For example, $S_A$ should include C, D, E, F, G, H — not just C and D. Missing H or F inflates $PW$ errors by 5+ points.

---

### Question B — Choice 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *A small manufacturing plant has 5 departments. The closeness ratings (1–6 scale) are given below. Apply the CORELAP algorithm to develop a layout. Compute the TCV and RTC. Comment on the quality of the layout.*

| Dept Pair | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | — | 6 | 4 | 5 | 2 |
| 2 | 6 | — | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| 3 | 4 | 5 | — | 6 | 1 |
| 4 | 5 | 3 | 6 | — | 4 |
| 5 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 4 | — |

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Compute TCR**

$$TCR_1 = 6+4+5+2 = 17$$

$$TCR_2 = 6+5+3+4 = 18$$

$$TCR_3 = 4+5+6+1 = 16$$

$$TCR_4 = 5+3+6+4 = 18$$

$$TCR_5 = 2+4+1+4 = 11$$

**Step 2: Tie at 18 between Dept 2 and Dept 4**

Tie-breaker — sum of top 3 relationships:
- Dept 2: 6+5+4 = 15
- Dept 4: 6+5+4 = 15

Use index → **Dept 2** as seed. Place at center.

```
.  .  .
.  2  .
.  .  .
```

**Step 3: Compute PS** (Placed = {2})

$$PS_1 = 6, \quad PS_3 = 5, \quad PS_4 = 3, \quad PS_5 = 4$$

**Dept 1** chosen (PS=6). Place adjacent to Dept 2.

```
.  1  .
.  2  .
.  .  .
```

**Step 4: PS update (Placed = {1, 2})**

$$PS_3 = 4+5 = 9, \quad PS_4 = 5+3 = 8, \quad PS_5 = 2+4 = 6$$

**Dept 3** chosen. Highest relationship with placed = Dept 2 (5) > Dept 1 (4). Place south of Dept 2:

```
.  1  .
.  2  .
.  3  .
```

**Step 5: PS update (Placed = {1, 2, 3})**

$$PS_4 = 5+3+6 = 14, \quad PS_5 = 2+4+1 = 7$$

**Dept 4** chosen. Highest = Dept 3 (6). Place east of Dept 3:

```
.  1  .
.  2  4
.  3  .
```

**Step 6: Place Dept 5**

PS = 2+4+1+4 = 11. Best partner = Dept 1 or Dept 4 (both rating 4). Place east of Dept 1:

```
.  1  5
.  2  4
.  3  .
```

Re-examine adjacencies: Dept 5 placed north-east. Dept 5 is now adjacent to Dept 1 (rating 4) and Dept 4 (rating 4). Dept 3 still has a slot east.

Actually, with 5 departments, only 5 of 9 cells are filled. Let me redo placement to maximize adjacencies.

A better layout would be:

```
.  1  5
2  .  4
.  3  .
```

Here adjacencies:
- 1–2: 6
- 1–4: 5
- 1–5: 2
- 2–4: 3
- 2–3: 5
- 3–4: 6
- 3–5: 1
- 4–5: 4

Side-adjacencies: 1–2, 1–5, 2–4, 2–3, 3–4, 4–5 → ratings 6, 2, 3, 5, 6, 4 = sum = 28
Diagonal-adjacencies: 1–3, 1–4, 2–5, 3–5 → ratings 4, 5, 4, 1 → contribution = (4+5+4+1)/2 = 7

$$TCV = 28 + 7 = 35$$

**Step 7: MPCS**

Sorted ratings: 6, 6, 5, 5, 4, 4, 4, 3, 2, 1. For 5 departments, $k = 1$ to 4:

$$MPCS = 1(6) + 2(6) + 3(5) + 4(5) = 6+12+15+20 = 53$$

**Step 8: RTC**

$$RTC = \frac{35}{53} \times 100 = 66.04\ \%$$

**Comment:** An RTC of **66%** indicates a good-quality layout. The high-rated pairs (1–2, 3–4) are placed adjacent as desired.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *side-adjacency* and *diagonal-adjacency* contributions in TCV. **Remember**: side-adjacent = full rating, diagonal = half rating. Skipping this distinction costs 2 marks instantly.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (print this box before the exam):**

- **Line Balancing**: assigns tasks to workstations respecting precedence and $C_t$.
- **Cycle time** $C_t = T_{avail} / R$.
- **Minimum workstations** $N_{min} = \lceil \sum t_i / C_t \rceil$.
- **Line efficiency** $\eta = \sum t_i / (N \cdot C_t) \times 100$.
- **Balance delay** $BD = 1 - \eta$.
- **Smoothness index** $SI = \sqrt{\sum_k (C_t - T_k)^2}$ (lower = better).
- **RPW method**: $PW_i = t_i + \sum_{j \in S_i} t_j$; rank descending; assign greedily.
- **CORELAP** = computerized relationship layout planning (Lee & Moore, 1967).
- **Closeness codes** (high to low): A=6, E=5, I=4, O=3, U=2, X=1.
- **TCR** of dept = sum of its closeness ratings with all other depts.
- **Highest TCR dept is the seed**, placed in the **center**.
- **Tie-breaker** rule: higher TCR; if still tied, lower index number.
- **TCV** = sum of side-adjacency ratings + (sum of diagonal ratings / 2).
- **MPCS** = $\sum_{k=1}^{m-1} k \cdot r_k$ where $r_k$ is the $k$-th highest rating.
- **RTC** = $TCV / MPCS \times 100$. A value $> 60\%$ is a *good* layout.
- **CORELAP** uses *qualitative* input (ARC); **CRAFT** uses *quantitative* (From-To Chart).
- **RPW is heuristic** — not guaranteed optimal; modern software (FlexSim, Siemens Plant Simulation) refines these classic algorithms.
